# Agents and Humans Are the Same Kind of Role

**Flexibility: Required**

## The belief

Agents and humans are governed as **the same kind of organizational role.** The
apparatus for designing, birthing, onboarding, managing, and reviewing a seat
applies identically to both. There is one work-packet schema, one birthing
process, and one org chart; a section may be marked N/A for a given fill, but it
is never absent by default just because the occupant is an agent or a person.

## Why we believe this

The value of "agents in an org chart" (JOS-C53) collapses if we maintain two
parallel systems — one rich process for agents and an ad-hoc one for humans (or
vice versa). Parity is what lets us reuse the proven human apparatus for agents
*and* upgrade human onboarding with the rigor we built for agents. It also keeps
the org chart coherent: a manager seat can be human while its reports are
agents, or the reverse, because they are the same kind of node. JOS-P01 already
commits to human/agent parity for trust; this Principle generalizes that to the
whole lifecycle.

## What this looks like in practice

- The context-bundle (human/venture onboarding) and the agent Context Pack
  converge into one Work-Packet schema.
- Birthing a human hire and birthing an agent run the same phases and produce
  the same artifact shapes.
- Org-chart placement, KPIs, trust ladder, and review are defined the same way
  regardless of who fills the seat.

## What this rules out

- A separate, richer creation process for agents than for human hires (or the
  reverse).
- Work-packet sections silently omitted "because it's an agent" / "because it's
  a person."
- Two registries / two org charts for the two kinds of occupant.

## Connected Objects

- **[JOS-C53]** Agents in an Org Chart (founds this Principle).
- **[JOS-P01]** Trust & Graduated Autonomy (parity for trust, generalized here).
- **[JOS-P04]** Mandatory Escalation (every role has a manager + owner).
- **[JOS-P26]** Every Role Births with a Complete Work Packet (one packet schema for both).
